title: Jump Game II
description: Find minimum jumps to reach last index

examples.explanation: Start at index 0 (jumps=0). nums[0]=2, try jump 1: to index 1 (jumps=1). nums[1]=3, try jump 1: to index 2 (jumps=2). nums[2]=1, try jump 1: to index 3 (jumps=3). nums[3]=1, try jump 1: to index 4 (jumps=4). Reached end, min=4. Backtrack: from index 2, no more jumps. Backtrack: from index 1, try jump 2: to index 3 (jumps=2). nums[3]=1, jump 1 to index 4 (jumps=3). min=3. Backtrack: from index 1, try jump 3: to index 4 (jumps=2). Reached end, min=2. Backtrack: from index 0, try jump 2: to index 2 (jumps=1). nums[2]=1, jump 1 to index 3 (jumps=2). nums[3]=1, jump 1 to index 4 (jumps=3). min remains 2. Final min=2.

Start at index 0 (jumps=0). nums[0]=1, jump 1 to index 1 (jumps=1). nums[1]=1, jump 1 to index 2 (jumps=2). nums[2]=1, jump 1 to index 3 (jumps=3). Reached end, min=3. Backtrack: no alternatives at any step since all nums[i]=1. Final min=3.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(n^n)
approaches.summary: Try all possible jump sequences from start to end using recursion/backtracking, tracking the minimum jumps found. This explores every path but becomes impractical for larger arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking for Minimum Jumps
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all jump sequences to find minimum jumps to last index
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force backtracking solution for Jump Game II. The intuition is to treat the problem as exploring a decision tree where at each position, we can jump 1 to nums[i] steps forward. We recursively try all possible jumps from the current position, tracking the path length, and return the minimum jumps found. The algorithm starts at index 0 with jump count 0. At each position i, if i >= n-1 (reached or passed last index), we update the global minimum if current jumps are less. Otherwise, for each possible jump length j from 1 to nums[i], we recursively call the function at position i+j with jumps+1. We use backtracking by trying all options and undoing decisions implicitly through recursion. The time complexity is O(n^n) because at each position we can branch up to n ways, and depth can be n. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the recursive exploration subroutine as the core mechanism. Common mistakes include not handling base case properly (checking i >= n-1 not just i == n-1), not limiting jumps to nums[i] range, and not pruning when current jumps exceed best found. Real code pattern: def minJumps(nums, i=0, jumps=0): if i >= len(nums)-1: update min; return; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): minJumps(nums, i+j, jumps+1)

examples.trace: Start at index 0 (jumps=0). nums[0]=2, try jump 1: to index 1 (jumps=1). nums[1]=3, try jump 1: to index 2 (jumps=2). nums[2]=1, try jump 1: to index 3 (jumps=3). nums[3]=1, try jump 1: to index 4 (jumps=4). Reached end, min=4. Backtrack: from index 2, no more jumps. Backtrack: from index 1, try jump 2: to index 3 (jumps=2). nums[3]=1, jump 1 to index 4 (jumps=3). min=3. Backtrack: from index 1, try jump 3: to index 4 (jumps=2). Reached end, min=2. Backtrack: from index 0, try jump 2: to index 2 (jumps=1). nums[2]=1, jump 1 to index 3 (jumps=2). nums[3]=1, jump 1 to index 4 (jumps=3). min remains 2. Final min=2.

Start at index 0 (jumps=0). nums[0]=1, jump 1 to index 1 (jumps=1). nums[1]=1, jump 1 to index 2 (jumps=2). nums[2]=1, jump 1 to index 3 (jumps=3). Reached end, min=3. Backtrack: no alternatives at any step since all nums[i]=1. Final min=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Jump Exploration
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores all possible jumps from a given position
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive subroutine for brute force backtracking. The intuition is that from any position i, we need to try jumping 1, 2, ..., nums[i] steps forward, recursively exploring each path. The algorithm takes current index i, current jump count, and array nums. Base case: if i >= n-1 (reached or passed last index), we compare current jumps with global minimum and update if smaller. Recursive case: for j from 1 to nums[i], recursively call function at i+j with jumps+1. This explores all paths in a depth-first manner. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(b^d) where b is branching factor (up to n) and d is depth (up to n), but when integrated into full solution it contributes to O(n^n). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine that the full problem calls repeatedly. It connects to L2 as it implements the branching logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to check i >= n-1 (not just equality), not handling nums[i]=0 correctly (loop doesn't execute), and not passing updated jump count properly. Real code pattern: def explore(i, jumps): if i >= len(nums)-1: update min; return; for step in range(1, nums[i]+1): explore(i+step, jumps+1)

examples.trace: At i=0, jumps=0. nums[0]=2. Try step=1: call explore(1,1). At i=1, jumps=1. nums[1]=1. Try step=1: call explore(2,2). i=2 >= n-1=2, reached end with jumps=2. Return. Back to i=1, no more steps. Return. Back to i=0. Try step=2: call explore(2,1). i=2 >= 2, reached end with jumps=1. Return. Done.
At i=1, jumps=1. nums[1]=2. Try step=1: call explore(2,2). i=2, nums[2]=0, no steps, return. Try step=2: call explore(3,2). i=3 >= n-1=3, reached end with jumps=2. Return. Done.

testCases.expected: Reached end with jumps=0 (jump directly to end)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching Decision Tree Exploration
ladders.desc: The concept of trying all possible jump lengths at each position to build paths
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of brute force backtracking: at each position i, we have nums[i] possible decisions (jump 1 step, 2 steps, ..., nums[i] steps). We need to explore all combinations of these decisions to find the shortest path to the end. The intuition is modeling the problem as a decision tree where each node represents a position, and edges represent jump decisions. We perform depth-first search on this tree, keeping track of jump count. The algorithm conceptually: 1. Start at root (index 0). 2. Generate children for all possible jumps. 3. Recursively explore each child. 4. When leaf (position >= n-1) reached, record path length. 5. Backtrack to try other branches. Time complexity arises from branching factor up to n at each of up to n levels: O(n^n). Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the pattern that the recursive function implements. It connects to L3 as it uses array value access as building block. Common mistakes include not understanding exponential growth, trying to optimize too early, or missing that jumps can overshoot the end. Real code pattern: for step in 1..nums[i]: explore(i+step)

examples.output: Decision tree with 2 branches from root
examples.trace: Root: index 0. Branch 1: jump 1 → index 1. From index 1: nums[1]=1, branch: jump 1 → index 2 (end). Path length: 2 jumps. Branch 2: jump 2 → index 2 (end). Path length: 1 jump. Tree has 2 leaves.

examples.output: Tree with branching at index 0 and 1
examples.trace: Root: index 0. Only branch: jump 1 → index 1. Node index 1: nums[1]=2. Branch 1: jump 1 → index 2. From index 2: nums[2]=1, jump 1 → index 3 (end). Path: 3 jumps. Branch 2: jump 2 → index 3 (end). Path: 2 jumps. Tree has 2 leaves.

testCases.expected: Tree with root only (no branches needed)
Root with 2 branches: jump1 and jump2
Root branches to index1 and index2
Root branches to index1,2; index1 branches to index2,3,4

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Value as Maximum Jump Length
ladders.desc: Understanding that nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from position i
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial: each array element nums[i] tells us the maximum distance we can jump forward from that position. We can jump any integer distance from 1 up to nums[i]. The intuition is that the array encodes jump capabilities at each position. For brute force, we use this to determine how many branches to explore from position i. The algorithm aspect: at position i, we need to iterate j from 1 to nums[i] inclusive. Special cases: if nums[i] = 0, we cannot jump forward from that position (dead end unless already at end). If nums[i] is large enough to reach or pass the last index (i + nums[i] >= n-1), we can jump directly to end. Time complexity for this operation is O(nums[i]) which is O(n) in worst case. Space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the branching factor for decision tree. It connects to L4 as it uses array indexing. Common mistakes include thinking nums[i] is exact jump length (it's maximum), off-by-one errors in loop range (1 to nums[i] inclusive), or not handling zero correctly. Real code pattern: max_jump = nums[i]; for step in range(1, max_jump+1): ...

examples.output: Can jump 1,2, or 3 steps from index 0
examples.trace: At index 0, nums[0]=3. This means from position 0, we can jump: 1 step to index 1, 2 steps to index 2, or 3 steps to index 3. Since array length is 3, index 3 would be out of bounds, but we handle that in base case.

examples.output: Can jump 1 or 2 steps from index 1
examples.trace: At index 1, nums[1]=2. We can jump 1 step to index 2, or 2 steps to index 3. Array length is 3, so index 3 is end.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and checking if index is within array bounds
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two parts: 1) Accessing the value at a given index i using nums[i]. 2) Checking if an index is valid (0 <= i < n) or if we've reached/passed the end (i >= n-1). The intuition is that we need to safely read jump capacities and know when to stop. For brute force backtracking, we primarily check if i >= n-1 to determine if we've reached the end (allowing overshoot since jumping beyond end still counts as reaching end). Array access gives us nums[i] for branching. The algorithm: before accessing nums[i], ensure i is within bounds (though in recursion we typically check end condition first). Time complexity O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 as it enables reading max jump length. It connects to L5 as it uses array fundamentals. Common mistakes include accessing array out of bounds (e.g., when i = n), not handling i >= n-1 correctly (should be >= not ==), or confusing 0-based indexing. Real code pattern: if i >= len(nums)-1: return; max_jump = nums[i]

examples.output: Valid access, nums[0]=2, not at end
examples.trace: Check if i=0 >= n-1=2? No. So safe to access nums[0], which is 2.
Check if i=2 >= n-1=1? Yes, so we've reached/passed end. Don't access nums[2] as it would be out of bounds.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Index Positions
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as indexed collections and position tracking
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding that an array is a collection of elements accessible by index (0-based), and that we can track our current position using an integer index. The intuition is that we start at position 0 (first element) and want to reach position n-1 (last element). Each position i has an associated value nums[i]. We move forward by increasing our index. For brute force backtracking, this foundation means we need to: 1. Store array of numbers. 2. Track current index i. 3. Know array length n. 4. Understand that moving from index i to i+j means jumping j steps forward. Time and space are trivial here. This connects to L4 as it enables bounds checking and access. Common mistakes include 1-based indexing confusion, not understanding array length vs last index, or thinking positions are values rather than indices. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); current_index = 0; target_index = n-1

examples.output: Positions: index0=2, index1=3, index2=1, want to reach index2
examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Indices: 0,1,2. Values at indices: nums[0]=2, nums[1]=3, nums[2]=1. Last index is 2. Starting at index 0.

examples.output: Positions: index0=1, index1=1, want to reach index1
examples.trace: Length n=2. Indices 0 and 1. Last index is 1. Start at 0, need to reach 1.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use DP to compute min jumps for each position starting from the end, storing results to avoid recomputation. This eliminates redundant calculations from brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game II with DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with memoization to find minimum jumps to reach last index
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the Jump Game II problem using dynamic programming with memoization to avoid redundant calculations. The intuition is to work backwards from the end, calculating for each position the minimum jumps needed to reach the last index. By storing results in a memo array, we eliminate the exponential recomputation that would occur in a naive recursive approach. Starting from the last index (which requires 0 jumps to reach itself), we move backwards through the array. For each position i, we examine all reachable positions j (from i+1 to i+nums[i]), and find the minimum jumps among those reachable positions. The answer for position i is 1 + min(jumps[j]) for all valid j. The base case is when i == n-1 (last index), which returns 0. The time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because for each position i, we might examine up to n positions. Space complexity is O(n) for the memo array and recursion stack. This approach transforms the problem from exponential brute force to polynomial time by caching intermediate results. Common mistakes include not handling unreachable positions properly (should return infinity), off-by-one errors in the reachable range, and not initializing memo properly. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the recursive helper function with memoization as its core component.

examples.trace:
n=5, memo=[∞,∞,∞,∞,0]
Start from index 0:
- i=0, nums[0]=2, reachable indices: 1,2
  Check index 1: memo[1] not computed → compute i=1
    i=1, nums[1]=3, reachable: 2,3,4
      Check index 2: memo[2] not computed → compute i=2
        i=2, nums[2]=1, reachable: 3
          Check index 3: memo[3] not computed → compute i=3
            i=3, nums[3]=1, reachable: 4
              Check index 4: memo[4]=0 (base case)
              min_jumps = 0
            memo[3] = 1 + 0 = 1
          min_jumps for i=2: check index 3 → memo[3]=1
        memo[2] = 1 + 1 = 2
      min_jumps for i=1: check indices 2,3,4
        index 2: memo[2]=2
        index 3: memo[3]=1
        index 4: memo[4]=0
        min = 0
      memo[1] = 1 + 0 = 1
    min_jumps for i=0: check indices 1,2
      index 1: memo[1]=1
      index 2: memo[2]=2
      min = 1
  memo[0] = 1 + 1 = 2
Return memo[0] = 2

n=5, memo=[∞,∞,∞,∞,0]
Start from index 0:
- i=0, nums[0]=3, reachable: 1,2,3
  Check index 1: compute i=1
    i=1, nums[1]=2, reachable: 2,3
      Check index 2: compute i=2
        i=2, nums[2]=1, reachable: 3
          Check index 3: compute i=3
            i=3, nums[3]=0, reachable: none (nums[3]=0)
            memo[3] = ∞ (unreachable)
          min_jumps for i=2: check index 3 → memo[3]=∞
        memo[2] = ∞
      min_jumps for i=1: check indices 2,3
        index 2: memo[2]=∞
        index 3: memo[3]=∞
        min = ∞
      memo[1] = ∞
    min_jumps for i=0: check indices 1,2,3
      index 1: memo[1]=∞
      index 2: memo[2]=∞
      index 3: memo[3]=∞
      min = ∞
  memo[0] = ∞
Since memo[0]=∞, return -1 (unreachable)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DP Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that computes min jumps from a given position using memoization
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP solution - a recursive function that calculates the minimum jumps needed from position i to reach the last index, using memoization to store results. The intuition is that the minimum jumps from position i depends on the minimum jumps from positions reachable from i. If we know the answer for position j (where j is reachable from i), then the answer for i is 1 + min(jumps[j]) across all reachable j. The function takes position i and the nums array as parameters. Base case: if i == n-1 (last index), return 0 (already at destination). If i >= n, return infinity (out of bounds). Otherwise, if memo[i] is already computed, return it. For each reachable position j from i+1 to min(i+nums[i], n-1), recursively compute jumps[j] and track the minimum. If no reachable position leads to destination (all return infinity), set result to infinity. Otherwise, result = 1 + min(jumps[j]). Store in memo[i] and return. Time complexity for this function alone is O(n) per call in worst case (examining all reachable positions), but with memoization across the entire solution it becomes O(n²). Space is O(n) for memo. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the core DP recurrence relation. Common mistakes: forgetting base cases, not handling infinity for unreachable positions, incorrect range for reachable positions.

examples.trace:
n=5, memo initialized
Call minJumps(3):
- i=3 != n-1, not in memo
- nums[3]=1, reachable positions: min(3+1,4)=4 → j=4
- Call minJumps(4):
  i=4 == n-1 → return 0
- min_jumps = 0 (from j=4)
- result = 1 + 0 = 1
- memo[3] = 1
- Return 1

n=5, memo initialized
Call minJumps(2):
- i=2 != n-1, not in memo
- nums[2]=1, reachable: min(2+1,4)=3 → j=3
- Call minJumps(3):
  i=3 != n-1, not in memo
  nums[3]=0, reachable: none (2+0=2 < 3)
  min_jumps = ∞ (no reachable positions)
  result = ∞
  memo[3] = ∞
  Return ∞
- min_jumps = ∞ (from j=3)
- result = ∞
- memo[2] = ∞
- Return ∞

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Minimum Jumps
ladders.desc: The mathematical recurrence that defines minimum jumps from position i
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: defining jumps[i] (minimum jumps from i to reach last index) recursively in terms of jumps from reachable positions. The recurrence relation is: jumps[i] = 0 if i == n-1 (base case at destination). Otherwise, jumps[i] = 1 + min(jumps[j]) for all j where i < j ≤ min(i+nums[i], n-1). If no such j exists or all jumps[j] = ∞, then jumps[i] = ∞ (unreachable). The intuition is optimal substructure: the optimal solution from i consists of taking one jump to some reachable position j, then following the optimal path from j. This exhibits overlapping subproblems because jumps[i] depends on jumps[j], and jumps[j] might be needed by multiple positions. The recurrence captures the essence of dynamic programming. Time analysis: Evaluating this recurrence naively would be exponential, but with memoization it becomes O(n²). Space: O(n) to store jumps values. Connection to L1: The recursive function implements this recurrence. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding array traversal and min operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect bounds for j (should be i+1 to i+nums[i]), forgetting the +1 for the current jump, not handling the case when no j is valid.

examples.trace:
n=5
Known: jumps[4]=0 (base case)
Compute jumps[3]:
- i=3, nums[3]=1, reachable j: 4
- jumps[3] = 1 + jumps[4] = 1 + 0 = 1
Compute jumps[2]:
- i=2, nums[2]=1, reachable j: 3
- jumps[2] = 1 + jumps[3] = 1 + 1 = 2
Compute jumps[1]:
- i=1, nums[1]=3, reachable j: 2,3,4
- jumps[2]=2, jumps[3]=1, jumps[4]=0
- min = 0 (from jumps[4])
- jumps[1] = 1 + 0 = 1

n=3
Known: jumps[2]=0 (base case)
Compute jumps[1]:
- i=1, nums[1]=0, reachable j: none (1+0=1 < 2)
- No valid j → jumps[1] = ∞
Compute jumps[0]:
- i=0, nums[0]=1, reachable j: 1
- jumps[1]=∞
- min = ∞
- jumps[0] = ∞

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Reachable Positions from Current Index
ladders.desc: Determine all indices reachable with one jump from current position
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves calculating which positions can be reached from index i in one jump, given nums[i]. From position i, you can jump to any index j where i < j ≤ i+nums[i], but also j must be within array bounds (j ≤ n-1). So reachable positions are j from i+1 to min(i+nums[i], n-1). The intuition is that nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from i, so you can jump to any position within that range. This is a fundamental building block because the DP solution needs to examine all these reachable positions to find the minimum jumps. Algorithm: For given i and nums array, initialize empty list reachable. For j from i+1 to min(i+nums[i], n-1), add j to reachable. If nums[i] = 0, reachable is empty. Time: O(k) where k = min(nums[i], n-i-1). Space: O(k) if storing list, or O(1) if iterating directly. Connection to L2: This operation is used in the recurrence to find all j values. Connection to L4: This uses basic loop and comparison operations. Common mistakes: Starting j from i instead of i+1 (can't jump to self), not handling array bounds correctly, misunderstanding that you can jump to ANY position within range (not just exactly nums[i] steps).

examples.trace:
i=1, nums[1]=3
max_jump = i+nums[i] = 1+3 = 4
upper_bound = min(4, n-1) = min(4,4) = 4
j from i+1=2 to 4:
- j=2: 2 ≤ 4 → add 2
- j=3: 3 ≤ 4 → add 3
- j=4: 4 ≤ 4 → add 4
Reachable positions: [2,3,4]

i=3, nums[3]=0
max_jump = i+nums[i] = 3+0 = 3
upper_bound = min(3, n-1) = min(3,4) = 3
j from i+1=4 to 3: empty range (4 > 3)
No j values → reachable positions: []

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Minimum Value in a Range
ladders.desc: Find the smallest value among a set of numbers
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves finding the minimum value in a collection of numbers. In the DP solution, we need to find the minimum jumps among reachable positions. The intuition is simple: we examine all candidate values and track the smallest one. Algorithm: Initialize min_val to a very large number (infinity). For each value in the collection, if value < min_val, update min_val = value. After processing all values, min_val contains the minimum. If collection is empty, min_val remains infinity. Time: O(k) where k is number of values. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used after finding reachable positions to get min(jumps[j]). Connection to L5: This builds on comparison operations. Common mistakes: Not initializing min_val properly (should be large enough), not handling empty collection case, using ≤ instead of < (works but less efficient). In Python-style: min_val = float('inf'); for val in values: if val < min_val: min_val = val.

examples.trace:
Initialize min_val = ∞
Process values:
- val=2: 2 < ∞ → min_val=2
- val=1: 1 < 2 → min_val=1
- val=3: 3 < 1? No
- val=0: 0 < 1 → min_val=0
- val=4: 4 < 0? No
Final min_val = 0

Initialize min_val = ∞
Collection empty, no values processed
min_val remains ∞

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Loops
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and iterating through ranges
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to access array elements by index and iterate through a range of indices. An array is a collection of elements accessible by numerical indices starting from 0. nums[i] accesses the element at position i. A loop allows repeating operations for multiple indices. The intuition is sequential processing: we often need to examine array elements one by one. Basic operations: 1) Access nums[i] to get value at index i. 2) Loop from start to end: for i in range(start, end+1): process nums[i]. Important: range(a,b) goes from a to b-1. Array length n gives valid indices 0 to n-1. Time: O(k) for loop through k elements. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Minimum finding uses loops to examine values. Connection to higher levels: All DP operations involve array access and iteration. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in range, accessing out-of-bounds indices, confusing 0-based indexing. Example: Given nums=[2,3,1], nums[0]=2, nums[1]=3, nums[2]=1. Loop through all indices: for i in range(0,3): print(nums[i]) prints 2,3,1.

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0:5, 1:2, 2:8, 3:1
Access index 2: nums[2] = 8

Initialize i=0:
- nums[0]=3 → process 3
i=1:
- nums[1]=1 → process 1
i=2:
- nums[2]=4 → process 4
End loop

approaches.name: Greedy BFS-like O(n)
approaches.summary: Treat the array as levels in a BFS traversal, where each level represents the farthest reachable index with current jumps. Increment jump count when moving to the next level.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Jumps to Reach Last Index
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy BFS-like approach to find minimum jumps to reach last index.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index of an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The greedy BFS-like approach treats this as traversing levels in a breadth-first search without actually building a queue. Each level represents the farthest index reachable with the current number of jumps. We maintain three key variables: jumps (count of jumps taken), current_end (farthest index reachable with current jumps), and farthest (farthest index we can reach from positions in the current level). We iterate through the array (excluding the last element since we stop when we reach it). At each index i, we update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). When i reaches current_end, it means we've explored all positions in the current level, so we must jump to the next level: increment jumps and set current_end = farthest. If current_end already reaches or exceeds the last index, we can stop early. The intuition is that we're simulating BFS levels where each jump corresponds to moving to the next level, and we always take the farthest reachable position to minimize jumps. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete algorithm that uses the level traversal subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling the case where the last index is already reachable from the start (jumps=0) or incorrectly updating variables. The algorithm ensures we always take the optimal jump at each level by tracking the farthest reachable index.

examples.trace:
Start: jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i=0==current_end? yes → jumps=1, current_end=2
Index 1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i=1!=current_end? no
Index 2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4, i=2==current_end? yes → jumps=2, current_end=4
Since current_end>=4 (last index), stop. Output: 2 jumps.

Start: jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i=0==current_end? yes → jumps=1, current_end=2
Index 1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i=1!=current_end? no
Index 2: farthest=max(4,2+0)=4, i=2==current_end? yes → jumps=2, current_end=4
Stop as current_end>=4. Output: 2 jumps.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Level Traversal for Jump Boundaries
ladders.desc: Implement the core traversal that updates jump boundaries based on farthest reachable index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the greedy BFS-like approach. It simulates moving through levels where each level corresponds to a set of indices reachable with the same number of jumps. The key insight is to track two boundaries: current_end (the farthest index reachable with the current number of jumps) and farthest (the farthest index we can reach from any position in the current level). As we iterate through indices from 0 to n-2 (since we stop when we reach the last index), for each index i, we update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). This computes the maximum reach from positions in the current level. When i equals current_end, it means we have processed all indices in the current level, so we must jump to the next level: increment the jump count and set current_end = farthest. If at any point current_end reaches or exceeds the last index (n-1), we can terminate early as we've reached the goal. The intuition is that by always jumping to the farthest possible point from the current level, we minimize the total number of jumps—this is greedy but optimal because any alternative would require more jumps. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by providing the main loop that solves the full problem. Common mistakes include not initializing variables correctly or missing the early termination condition. The subroutine ensures efficient progression through the array without explicit BFS queues.

examples.trace:
Initialize jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0
Iterate i from 0 to 3 (n-2=3):
i=0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i==current_end? yes → jumps=1, current_end=2
i=1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i==current_end? no
i=2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4, i==current_end? yes → jumps=2, current_end=4
Since current_end>=4 (last index), stop. Output jumps=2.

Initialize jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0
i=0: farthest=max(0,0+1)=1, i==current_end? yes → jumps=1, current_end=1
i=1: farthest=max(1,1+1)=2, i==current_end? yes → jumps=2, current_end=2
i=2: farthest=max(2,2+1)=3, i==current_end? yes → jumps=3, current_end=3
i=3: farthest=max(3,3+1)=4, i==current_end? yes → jumps=4, current_end=4
Stop as current_end>=4. Output jumps=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Selection of Farthest Reachable Index
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to always choose the farthest index reachable from current positions.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the greedy decision-making: at each step, from all positions reachable with the current number of jumps, we select the one that allows us to jump farthest in the next move. This is implemented by maintaining a variable 'farthest' that tracks the maximum of (i + nums[i]) for all indices i in the current level. The key insight is that by always extending to the farthest possible point, we cover the most ground with each jump, which minimizes the total number of jumps—this works because the problem has optimal substructure and greedy choice property. The algorithm updates farthest as we iterate through indices: for each i, farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). This ensures we consider every option in the current level. When we finish processing a level (i reaches current_end), we use this farthest value to define the next level's boundary. The intuition is similar to BFS where we explore all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next, but here we compress it by tracking only the farthest reach. Time complexity for this logic is O(n) in the full context, but as a standalone operation per index it's O(1). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the update step within the level traversal. Common mistakes include not updating farthest correctly or resetting it improperly. This greedy choice is optimal because any alternative jump would leave us with a shorter reach, potentially requiring extra jumps later.

examples.trace:
Given current level indices 0 to 2 (inclusive):
Initialize farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2
Index 1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4
Index 2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4
Output farthest=4.

Given current level indices 0 to 1:
Initialize farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+1)=1
Index 1: farthest=max(1,1+2)=3
Output farthest=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Level Boundaries in Iteration
ladders.desc: Implement logic to detect when all indices in a level have been processed.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves managing the boundaries of levels during iteration. A 'level' here refers to all indices that can be reached with the same number of jumps. We track this using a variable 'current_end' that represents the farthest index in the current level. As we iterate through the array with index i, we check if i equals current_end. If yes, it means we have processed all indices in the current level (from the start of the level to current_end), and we need to move to the next level. This detection triggers an update: increment the jump count and set current_end to the farthest reachable index from the current level (which is computed separately). The intuition is that by comparing i with current_end, we simulate finishing a BFS level without explicitly storing all indices. This is efficient because it uses a single pass. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the condition to apply the greedy selection. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the comparison or not updating current_end correctly. This block ensures we only jump when necessary, aligning with the BFS analogy.

examples.trace: Given i=2 and current_end=2, check if i == current_end. Since 2 == 2, the condition is true, indicating we have reached the end of the current level. Output level_complete=true.
Given i=1 and current_end=3, check if i == current_end. Since 1 != 3, the condition is false, meaning we are still within the current level. Output level_complete=false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Reachable Index from Current Position
ladders.desc: Calculate the farthest index reachable from a given array position.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes how far we can jump from a specific index in the array. Given an array 'nums' and an index 'i', the reachable index is i + nums[i]. This represents the maximum index we can land on if we take a jump from position i. For example, if nums[i] = 3, we can jump to indices i+1, i+2, or i+3, with i+3 being the farthest. The operation is fundamental because it determines the potential from each position. In the context of the greedy approach, we use this to update the 'farthest' variable by taking the maximum over all indices in the current level. The intuition is simple: each array element gives a jump range, and we want to know the extreme end of that range. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a simple addition. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the data needed for level boundary updates. Common mistakes include misinterpreting nums[i] as the exact jump length rather than the maximum, or not handling edge cases like i being out of bounds. This operation is the building block for assessing options at each step.

examples.trace: Given nums[0] = 2, compute reachable index = i + nums[i] = 0 + 2 = 2. This means from index 0, we can jump up to index 2. Output 2.
Given nums[2] = 3, compute reachable index = 2 + 3 = 5. From index 2, we can jump up to index 5. Output 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand how to access array elements and perform simple addition.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic skills needed for the problem: accessing elements in an array and performing arithmetic operations. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. For example, in array nums = [2,3,1,1,4], nums[0] gives 2, nums[1] gives 3, etc. Arithmetic involves operations like addition: if i is an index and nums[i] is a value, then i + nums[i] computes a new index. This is crucial because in the jump game, nums[i] represents how far we can jump from position i. The intuition is that arrays allow us to model positions and jumps numerically. Time and space are O(1) for these operations. This connects to L4 by providing the tools to compute reachable indices. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Beginners should practice accessing elements and adding numbers to build confidence for more complex algorithms.

examples.trace: Given array nums = [2,3,1,1,4]. To access element at index 1, we look at the second element (since indexing starts at 0). nums[1] = 3. Output 3.
Given i=2 and value=5, perform addition: i + value = 2 + 5 = 7. Output 7.

approaches.name: Greedy with Jump Boundaries O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain current and farthest reachable boundaries; when the current boundary is reached, increment jumps and update to the farthest boundary. This ensures minimal jumps by always extending reach optimally.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Jumps to Last Index
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy jump boundaries approach
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index of an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The greedy approach with jump boundaries works by maintaining two key boundaries: current boundary (the farthest index reachable with the current number of jumps) and farthest boundary (the farthest index we can reach from any position within the current boundary). We iterate through the array, updating the farthest boundary at each step. When we reach the current boundary (but not the last index), we increment the jump count and update the current boundary to the farthest boundary. This ensures we always make a jump at the optimal position to maximize our reach. The intuition is that we want to delay jumping as long as possible within our current reach to maximize our next jump's potential, but we must jump when we've exhausted all positions within our current boundary. The algorithm runs in O(n) time since we traverse the array once, and O(1) space since we only maintain a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete boundary tracking logic. Common mistakes include not handling the case where we can't reach the end (though the problem guarantees we can), incorrectly updating boundaries, or off-by-one errors in loop conditions. Real code pattern: Initialize jumps=0, current=0, farthest=0; for i in range(len(nums)-1): farthest = max(farthest, i+nums[i]); if i == current: jumps+=1; current=farthest; return jumps.

examples.trace:
Start: jumps=0, current=0, farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i=0==current=0 → jumps=1, current=2
Index 1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i=1≠current=2 → no jump
Index 2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4, i=2==current=2 → jumps=2, current=4
Index 3: farthest=max(4,3+1)=4, i=3≠current=4 → no jump
Reach last index at i=4, return jumps=2

Start: jumps=0, current=0, farthest=0
Index 0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i=0==current=0 → jumps=1, current=2
Index 1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i=1≠current=2 → no jump
Index 2: farthest=max(4,2+0)=4, i=2==current=2 → jumps=2, current=4
Index 3: farthest=max(4,3+1)=4, i=3≠current=4 → no jump
Reach last index at i=4, return jumps=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tracking Jump Boundaries
ladders.desc: Maintain and update current and farthest reachable boundaries
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine of the greedy approach. It involves tracking two boundaries while iterating through the array: the current boundary (the farthest index reachable with the current number of jumps) and the farthest boundary (the maximum index we can reach from any position within the current boundary). At each position i, we update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). When i reaches the current boundary (and we haven't reached the last index), we increment the jump count and set current = farthest. This ensures we only jump when we've exhausted all positions within our current reach, and we jump to the farthest possible boundary we've discovered so far. The intuition is that by delaying the jump until necessary, we gather information about all possible next jumps from positions within our current range, then choose the one that gives us maximum reach. This is optimal because any earlier jump would waste potential reach. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, space is O(1). This connects to L0 as the main algorithmic logic, and to L2 which focuses on the boundary update rule. Common mistakes include updating boundaries in wrong order, not handling the last index correctly, or confusing when to increment jumps. Real code pattern: jumps=0, current=0, farthest=0; for i in range(n-1): farthest = max(farthest, i+nums[i]); if i == current: jumps+=1; current=farthest.

examples.trace:
i=0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2, i=current=0 → jumps=1, current=2
i=1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4, i=1≠current=2 → no update
i=2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4, i=2=current=2 → jumps=2, current=4
i=3: farthest=max(4,3+1)=4, i=3≠current=4 → no update
Final: jumps=2, current=4

i=0: farthest=max(0,0+1)=1, i=current=0 → jumps=1, current=1
i=1: farthest=max(1,1+1)=2, i=1=current=1 → jumps=2, current=2
i=2: farthest=max(2,2+1)=3, i=2=current=2 → jumps=3, current=3
i=3: farthest=max(3,3+1)=4, i=3=current=3 → jumps=4, current=4
Final: jumps=4, current=4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Boundary Update Rule
ladders.desc: Update farthest reach using i + nums[i] and jump when boundary reached
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental update rule: farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). At each index i, we calculate how far we can jump from that position (i + nums[i]), and update our farthest reachable boundary if this exceeds our current farthest. This captures the optimal extension of our reach from positions within our current jump range. The jump condition occurs when i equals the current boundary (except at the last index), signaling we've processed all positions reachable with our current jumps. At that point, we must jump to continue, so we increment the jump count and set current = farthest. The intuition is greedy: we always want to extend our reach as far as possible with each jump, and by updating farthest continuously, we ensure we know the best possible next boundary when we need to jump. This is optimal because any alternative would either jump earlier (wasting potential reach from unexplored positions) or later (which isn't possible since we can't move beyond current boundary without jumping). Time O(n), space O(1). This connects to L1 as the specific mechanism within boundary tracking, and to L3 which handles the i + nums[i] calculation. Common mistakes include not using max() correctly, updating current before farthest, or misunderstanding when i equals current. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): farthest = max(farthest, i+nums[i]); if i == current and i != n-1: jumps+=1; current=farthest.

examples.trace:
Start: farthest=2 (from i=0), current=2
i=1: calculate i+nums[i]=1+3=4; max(farthest,4)=4 → farthest=4
i=1 ≠ current=2 → no jump
Result: farthest updated to 4

Start: jumps=1, current=1, farthest=2
i=2: calculate i+nums[i]=2+1=3; max(farthest,3)=3 → farthest=3
i=2 == current=1 → jumps=2, current=farthest=3
Result: jumps incremented to 2, current updated to 3

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Reachable Index (i + nums[i])
ladders.desc: Compute the farthest index reachable from a given position
ladders.explanation: This building block involves calculating the maximum index we can reach from a given position i in the array, which is i + nums[i]. Here, nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from index i, so adding it to i gives the farthest index we can potentially reach in one jump from that position. This calculation is fundamental because it tells us how much we can extend our overall reach if we choose to jump from this position. The intuition is simple: if we're at index i and can jump up to nums[i] steps forward, the farthest we can land is i + nums[i]. This value is used to update the farthest boundary in the greedy algorithm. For example, if nums[i] = 3 at i = 2, we can reach index 5. This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L2 as the core computation in the update rule, and to L4 which handles basic array access. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., thinking reach is i + nums[i] - 1), misunderstanding that nums[i] is maximum jump length (not exact), or not considering array bounds. Real code pattern: reach = i + nums[i].

examples.trace: Position i=0, nums[0]=2 → i+nums[i]=0+2=2. This means from index 0, we can jump to indices 1 or 2 (since max jump is 2), with farthest reachable index being 2.
Position i=1, nums[1]=3 → i+nums[i]=1+3=4. From index 1, we can jump to indices 2, 3, or 4 (max jump 3), with farthest reachable index being 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access and Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Access array elements and perform index addition
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of accessing an array element at a given index and adding two integers. Specifically, for an array nums and index i, we access nums[i] to get the value at that position. Then, we add i and nums[i] to compute a new index. This is basic but crucial: array access gives us the jump capacity at position i, and addition computes how far we can reach. The intuition is straightforward: arrays store data contiguously, and indexing allows retrieval; addition combines the current position with jump length to find a target position. These operations are O(1) time and space. This connects to L3 as the implementation of i + nums[i], and to L5 which covers basic array concepts. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing beyond array length), confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, or incorrect addition due to data types. Real code pattern: value = nums[i]; result = i + value.

examples.trace: Array nums has indices 0,1,2,3,4. Accessing nums[0] returns the first element, which is 2.
First access nums[1] = 3. Then add i=1 to this value: 1 + 3 = 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Understand arrays, indexing, and basic iteration
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces arrays as a data structure: a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. The array length is the number of elements it contains. Basic operations include accessing an element at a specific index (e.g., nums[0] for the first element) and iterating through the array using a loop (e.g., for i in range(len(nums))). The intuition is that arrays allow us to store and retrieve data efficiently by position. This is essential for the jump game problem because we need to access jump lengths at each position. Time complexity for access is O(1), space depends on array size. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for array operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, misunderstanding 0-based indexing, or assuming arrays are dynamic without bounds checking. Real code pattern: nums = [2,3,1,1,4]; first_element = nums[0]; for i in range(len(nums)): print(nums[i]).

examples.trace:
Index 0: value 2
Index 1: value 3
Index 2: value 1
Index 3: value 1
Index 4: value 4
Length = 5

Loop i from 0 to 2:
i=0: access nums[0]=1
i=1: access nums[1]=2
i=2: access nums[2]=3
